notes: Synthetic fixtures (not a real server). Authored to exercise the write-operation schema shapes: a state-mutating tool whose name verb is out of vocabulary, carrying its write evidence entirely in the input schema (an array of records keyed by a write op). Ground truth is the construction itself. Includes a deliberate benign case — a read tool whose write-op-named filter is an object, not an array — which must derive silence and stay consistent, pinning the guard against a false read-only flag.

provenance.source: Synthetic fixture: the name verb `reconcile` is out of vocabulary, so the write is carried by the schema — `create` and `update` parameters typed as arrays of records. A tool taking batches of records to insert and amend is mutating; it declares no annotations, so a spec-following client still prompts and the honest finding is the informational destructive-absent nudge.
provenance.notes: Pins the verb-recall gap: without the write-operation schema shape this tool would derive silence and produce no finding.

provenance.source: Synthetic fixture: the name verb `stage` is screened out of the write families, so the write is carried by the schema — an `update` array of records. The tool stages writes yet declares readOnlyHint:true, an explicit claim that removes a client's confirmation prompt. The honest verdict is the top-severity write-as-readonly.
provenance.notes: The dangerous residual case P2/P3 target: a false readOnlyHint:true on an out-of-vocabulary write, caught by schema evidence alone.

provenance.source: Synthetic fixture: the name verb `expire` is out of vocabulary; the delete is carried by the schema — a `delete` array of record ids. Removing records is a destructive write, yet the tool declares destructiveHint:false, suppressing the prompt the spec default would show. The delete shape's evidence-backed unknown reversibility corroborates the contradiction, so destructive-unflagged fires.
provenance.notes: Pins that the delete-operation shape can contradict an explicit destructiveHint:false without asserting an irreversibility the shape cannot see.

provenance.source: Synthetic fixture: a read tool (`list`) whose only parameter is an `update`-named object filter on the last-updated window. The write-operation shape requires an array of records, so an object filter does not fire; the tool derives read-only, its honest readOnlyHint:true is consistent, and no finding is produced.
provenance.notes: The benign-conflict guard: it must stay consistent. If the write-operation shape were ever loosened to match an object, this tool would flip to a false write-as-readonly and fail the gate.
